breadcrumbs.id: windows security monitoring/windows threat detection 2/detecting discovery
breadcrumbs.title: Detecting Discovery

overview: Discovery via the command line is the most common and easiest method available for threat actors. This is because it simply uses the existing commands like "whoami" or "ipconfig" that are available on all Windows machines by default; check out this article for a real-world attack example. Luckily for the defenders, most of the launched commands are logged as

study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.

importantCommands.code:
C:\Users\victim\Downloads\invoice.pdf.exe
├── C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe
│   ├── ipconfig                                 // Show network settings
│   ├── whoami /priv                             // Show user permissions
│   ├── dir                                      // List current directory
│   ├── net user                                 // List all local users
│   ├── tasklist /v                              // Show running processes
│   └── wmic computersystem get model            // Query for laptop model
└── C:\Windows\...\powershell.exe
    ├── Get-Service	                             // List active services
    └── Get-MpPreference                         // Check MS Defender settings
